---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 4: The single-equation linear model and OLS (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

**Source.** Stata examples reproduced from
[UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 4](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-4-the-single-equation-linear-model-and-ols-estimation/).

**Book.** Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel Data*,
MIT Press. Chapter 4: *The Single-Equation Linear Model and OLS Estimation*.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

The datasets are bundled with `StatEcon` and load by name.

```{julia}
mroz    = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("mroz"))      # 753 x 22
nls80   = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("nls80"))     # 935 x 17
jtrain1 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("jtrain1"))   # 471 x 30
size(mroz), size(nls80), size(jtrain1)
```

Stata's `predict r, res` after a `regress` — residuals aligned to the rows of
the original data frame, `missing` wherever the fit dropped the observation.

```{julia}
predict_res(m, df, y) = df[!, y] .- FixedEffectModels.predict(m, df)
```

# Example 4.1 on page 59 (mroz.dta)

## OLS wage equation

```{julia}
#=
use mroz, clear

reg lwage exper expersq educ age kidslt6 kidsge6
=#
ols41 = stata_regress(mroz; y = :lwage,
                      x = [:exper, :expersq, :educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6])
```

Stata prints `N = 428`, `F(6, 421) = 13.19`, `R² = 0.1582`, `Root MSE = .66823`,
and `educ = .1078319 (.0144021)`. The Julia fit reproduces every published
coefficient, standard error, *t*, and confidence limit to the printed digits.

## Joint test on the demographic variables

```{julia}
#=
test  age kidslt6 kidsge6
=#
stata_test(ols41, ["age", "kidslt6", "kidsge6"])
```

Stata: `F(3, 421) = 0.24`, `Prob > F = 0.8705`. Julia: `F = 0.2370`,
`p = 0.8705` — identical (Stata rounds 0.237 to 0.24).

## Heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors

```{julia}
#=
reg lwage exper expersq educ age kidslt6 kidsge6, robust
=#
ols41r = stata_regress(mroz; y = :lwage,
                       x = [:exper, :expersq, :educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6],
                       vce = :robust)
```

```{julia}
#=
di .316*(428/(428-6))^.5
.31823852
=#
Printf.@printf("%.8f\n", 0.316 * (428 / (428 - 6))^0.5)
```

Stata's `, robust` applies the finite-sample factor `N/(N-k)`; `FixedEffectModels`
does the same, so the robust SEs match exactly
(`educ`: `.0136235`, `_cons`: `.3183346`). The UCLA note explains that the book
reports the uncorrected version — `.316 * sqrt(428/422) = .31823852` recovers
Stata's intercept SE, and the Julia arithmetic gives the same number.

## LM test via the residual regression

```{julia}
#=
reg lwage exper expersq educ

predict res, res

reg res exper expersq educ age kidslt6 kidsge6

di 428*.0017
.7276

di chi2tail(3, .728)
.86659913
=#
ols41s = stata_regress(mroz; y = :lwage, x = [:exper, :expersq, :educ])
```

```{julia}
mroz.res = predict_res(ols41s, mroz, :lwage)   # missing where lwage is missing
Printf.@printf("(%d missing values generated)\n", sum(ismissing, mroz.res))

aux41 = stata_regress(mroz; y = :res,
                      x = [:exper, :expersq, :educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6])
```

```{julia}
LM41 = StatsBase.nobs(aux41) * StatsBase.r2(aux41)
Printf.@printf("LM  = n*R2      = %.4f\n", LM41)
Printf.@printf("p   = chi2tail(3, LM) = %.8f\n", 1 - cdf(Chisq(3), LM41))
Printf.@printf("Stata's rounded version: 428*.0017 = %.4f, p = %.8f\n",
               428 * 0.0017, 1 - cdf(Chisq(3), 0.728))
```

The short regression, the auxiliary regression and its `R² = 0.0017` all match the
published output exactly. Stata's `di 428*.0017 = .7276` uses the four-decimal
printed `R²`; the exact `n·R²` is 0.7275, and either way the χ²(3) p-value is
about 0.867 — no evidence against the exclusion restrictions.

# Example 4.1 (continued) on page 60

The book's alternative LM statistic regresses a constant on the products of the
short-regression residual with the residuals from regressing each excluded
variable on the included ones.

```{julia}
#=
reg lwage exper expersq educ

predict u, res

reg age exper expersq educ

predict r1, res
reg kidslt6 exper expersq educ

predict r2, res
reg kidsge6 exper expersq educ

predict r3, res
gen one = 1
gen rage = u*r1
gen rle6 = u*r2
gen rge6 = u*r3

reg one rage rle6 rge6, nocons

di 428*0.0012
.5136

di chi2tail(3, .5136)
.91589355
=#
mroz.u = predict_res(ols41s, mroz, :lwage)

m_age = stata_regress(mroz; y = :age, x = [:exper, :expersq, :educ])
mroz.r1 = predict_res(m_age, mroz, :age)
```

```{julia}
m_k6 = stata_regress(mroz; y = :kidslt6, x = [:exper, :expersq, :educ])
mroz.r2 = predict_res(m_k6, mroz, :kidslt6)
```

```{julia}
m_kg6 = stata_regress(mroz; y = :kidsge6, x = [:exper, :expersq, :educ])
mroz.r3 = predict_res(m_kg6, mroz, :kidsge6)
```

```{julia}
mroz.one  = ones(DataFrames.nrow(mroz))
mroz.rage = mroz.u .* mroz.r1
mroz.rle6 = mroz.u .* mroz.r2
mroz.rge6 = mroz.u .* mroz.r3

nocons41 = stata_regress(mroz; y = :one, x = [:rage, :rle6, :rge6], nocons = true)
```

```{julia}
LM41b = StatsBase.nobs(nocons41) * StatsBase.r2(nocons41)
Printf.@printf("n*R2 (uncentered) = %.6f\n", LM41b)
Printf.@printf("p                 = %.8f\n", 1 - cdf(Chisq(3), LM41b))
Printf.@printf("Stata: 428*0.0012 = %.4f, chi2tail(3, .5136) = %.8f\n",
               428 * 0.0012, 1 - cdf(Chisq(3), 0.5136))
```

All three auxiliary regressions (N = 753 each) reproduce the published tables
exactly, and the no-constant regression gives
`rage = -.0028426 (.0109138)`, `rle6 = -.0940857 (.1710924)`,
`rge6 = -.0266196 (.059492)` with uncentered `R² = 0.0012` — the same as Stata.
The LM statistic is 0.5215 (Stata's `428*0.0012 = .5136` uses the rounded `R²`),
p ≈ 0.914.

# Example 4.3 on page 63 (nls80.dta)

```{julia}
#=
use nls80, clear

reg lwage exper tenure married south urban black educ
=#
ols43a = stata_regress(nls80; y = :lwage,
                       x = [:exper, :tenure, :married, :south, :urban, :black, :educ])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg lwage exper tenure married south urban black educ iq
=#
ols43b = stata_regress(nls80; y = :lwage,
                       x = [:exper, :tenure, :married, :south, :urban, :black, :educ, :iq])
```

Both regressions match the published output digit for digit: without `iq`,
`educ = .0654307 (.0062504)` and `R² = 0.2526`; adding `iq` lowers the return to
schooling to `.0544106 (.0069285)` with `iq = .0035591 (.0009918)` and
`R² = 0.2628`.

# Example 4.4 on page 66 (jtrain1.dta)

```{julia}
#=
gen scrap88 = (year==1988 & scrap~=.)
reg lscrap grant if scrap88
=#
jtrain1.scrap88 = (jtrain1.year .== 1988) .& .!ismissing.(jtrain1.scrap)
jt88 = jtrain1[jtrain1.scrap88, :]

ols44a = stata_regress(jt88; y = :lscrap, x = [:grant])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg lscrap grant lscrap_1 if scrap88
=#
ols44b = stata_regress(jt88; y = :lscrap, x = [:grant, :lscrap_1])
```

Both fits reproduce the published tables exactly (N = 54). Without the lag,
`grant = .0566004 (.4055519)`; conditioning on `lscrap_1` the grant effect turns
negative, `-.2539697 (.1470311)`, with `lscrap_1 = .8311606 (.0444444)` and
`R² = 0.8728`.

# Example 4.5 on page 69 (nls80.dta)

```{julia}
#=
use nls80, clear

gen educ_iq = educ*iq
reg lwage exper tenure married south urban black educ iq educ_iq
=#
nls80.educ_iq = nls80.educ .* nls80.iq

ols45 = stata_regress(nls80; y = :lwage,
                      x = [:exper, :tenure, :married, :south, :urban,
                           :black, :educ, :iq, :educ_iq])
```

```{julia}
#=
test iq  educ_iq
=#
stata_test(ols45, ["iq", "educ_iq"])
```

The interacted model matches exactly (`educ_iq = .0003399 (.0003826)`,
`R² = 0.2634`), and the joint test gives `F(2, 925) = 6.83`, `Prob > F = 0.0011`
— identical to Stata.

# Verification summary

| Example | Quantity | Stata | Julia | Match |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4.1 | `educ` coef / SE | .1078319 / .0144021 | .1078319 / .0144021 | yes |
| 4.1 | `exper` coef / SE | .039819 / .013393 | .039819 / .013393 | yes |
| 4.1 | R² / Root MSE | 0.1582 / .66823 | 0.1582 / .66823 | yes |
| 4.1 | `test age kidslt6 kidsge6` | F(3,421)=0.24, p=0.8705 | F=0.2370, p=0.8705 | yes |
| 4.1 robust | `educ` SE | .0136235 | .0136235 | yes |
| 4.1 robust | `_cons` SE | .3183346 | .3183346 | yes |
| 4.1 LM | auxiliary R² | 0.0017 | 0.0017 | yes |
| 4.1 (cont.) | `rage` coef / SE | -.0028426 / .0109138 | -.0028426 / .0109138 | yes |
| 4.1 (cont.) | no-cons R² | 0.0012 | 0.0012 | yes |
| 4.3 | `educ` (no iq) | .0654307 / .0062504 | .0654307 / .0062504 | yes |
| 4.3 | `educ`, `iq` (with iq) | .0544106, .0035591 | .0544106, .0035591 | yes |
| 4.4 | `grant` (no lag) | .0566004 / .4055519 | .0566004 / .4055519 | yes |
| 4.4 | `grant`, `lscrap_1` | -.2539697, .8311606 | -.2539697, .8311606 | yes |
| 4.5 | `educ_iq` coef / SE | .0003399 / .0003826 | .0003399 / .0003826 | yes |
| 4.5 | `test iq educ_iq` | F(2,925)=6.83, p=0.0011 | F=6.8318, p=0.0011 | yes |

Every estimation on the UCLA Chapter 4 page reproduces exactly. The only
cosmetic differences are that `stata_test` prints the *F* statistic to four
decimals where Stata prints two, and that Stata's `di 428*.0017` /
`di 428*0.0012` computations of the LM statistic use the four-decimal rounded
`R²` values rather than the exact ones.
